title: On Zimmer's conjecture
speaker: Sebastian Hurtado-Salazar
abstract: The group $\mathrm{SL}_n(\mathbb Z)$ (when $n > 2$) is very rigid, for example, Margulis proved all its linear representations come from representations of $\mathrm{SL}_n(\mathbb R)$ and are as simple as one can imagine. Zimmer's conjecture states that certain "non-linear" representations ( group actions by diffeomorphisms on a closed manifold) come also from simple algebraic constructions.

proof_stages.label: Lie-group and lattice rigidity template
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up simple Lie groups and lattices, using SL_n as the running model, and recalls Mostow rigidity and Margulis superrigidity as the algebraic rigidity paradigm behind Zimmer’s conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Zimmer program and finite-image theorem
proof_stages.summary: Zimmer’s philosophy is formulated for actions of higher-rank lattices on closed manifolds, standard algebraic actions are described, and the Brown–Fisher–Hurtado theorem is stated: if dim M < n-1 then any smooth SL_n(Z)-type action has finite image.

proof_stages.label: Cocycle-superrigidity metric criterion
proof_stages.summary: Zimmer cocycle superrigidity is presented as producing a measurable invariant Riemannian metric in the low-dimensional setting, and the speaker explains that upgrading this metric to a smooth one would force the desired finite-image conclusion via isometric rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Suspension and fiber derivative cocycle
proof_stages.summary: The original lattice action is replaced by the suspension (G x M)/Gamma with a G-action, so that invariant measures, ergodicity, metrics, and the derivative cocycle become fiberwise objects on a G-space.

proof_stages.label: Invariant metric from averaging and strong property T
proof_stages.summary: The proof aims to average an arbitrary fiber metric over G; a subexponential derivative-growth theorem gives the operator bounds needed to apply Lafforgue strong property T to a Sobolev space of metrics and obtain a G-invariant fiber metric.

proof_stages.label: Exponential derivative growth yields Lyapunov measures
proof_stages.summary: To prove the subexponential-growth theorem, the negation is converted into hyperbolic behavior and then into an A-invariant measure on the suspension with a nonzero Lyapunov exponent, using standard dynamical arguments and Oseledets theory.

proof_stages.label: Higher-rank measure rigidity contradiction
proof_stages.summary: A higher-rank measure-rigidity theorem for the Cartan A-action upgrades the A-invariant Lyapunov measure, provided it projects to Haar measure, to a G-invariant measure, contradicting Zimmer cocycle superrigidity’s measurable-isometric conclusion.
